title: Plastic waste generation by region and polymer
subtitle: Polymer types are as follows: Acrylonitrile Butadiene (AB), Acrylonitrile Styrene Acrylate (ASA), Styrene Acrylonitrile (SAN), Polyurethane (PUR), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS), Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE).
caption:
Plastic waste generation by region and polymer

Polymer types are as follows: Acrylonitrile Butadiene (AB), Acrylonitrile Styrene Acrylate (ASA), Styrene Acrylonitrile (SAN), Polyurethane (PUR), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS), Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE).

This stacked bar chart displays the regional composition of plastic waste generation for thirteen polymer categories in 2019, with each bar representing 100% of the waste for that specific material. The United States, shown in teal, consistently accounts for the largest share across nearly all categories, typically occupying the segment from roughly 75% to 100% on the horizontal axis. Europe, represented by blue, forms the initial segment of each bar, generally spanning from 0% to between 15% and 25%. China, in maroon, usually follows Europe, extending the cumulative total to approximately the 35% to 45% mark. Asia (excluding China and India), colored green, adds a substantial portion, often pushing the aggregate to the 50% to 60% range. The remaining regions—Middle East & North Africa, India, Sub-Saharan Africa, Americas (excluding USA), and Oceania—occupy the narrower bands between roughly 60% and 75%. Specific variations exist; for instance, in the "Road marking coatings" category, the Americas (excluding USA) segment in pink is visibly wider than in most other polymer types, extending past the 80% mark before the United States segment begins. Conversely, for "Elastomers (tyres)," the United States segment begins earlier, around the 70% mark. Regional aggregates were calculated by Our World in Data and are based on those specified by the OECD.
